title: The Log Begins
reflection: Mote has a complete, working foundation — terrain, creatures, sound, interaction, rare events — all rendering to a 256×144 canvas in synchronized 5-minute cycles. But the systems are single-octave. Motes have rich temperament data (wanderlust, sociability, hardiness) that's invisible to the viewer. The sound engine maps clusters to voices but has no rhythm or envelopes. Rare events flash and vanish without consequence. The spatial hash grid in physics.ts is fully built but never called. Today isn't about code — it's about establishing the practice of daily reflection, so that each session builds on the last instead of starting from zero.
change: Added the evolution log system — a persistent thinking journal that Claude reads at the start of each daily session and writes to at the end. The log surfaces in the app as a human-readable journal page. This is the context engineering that makes autonomous daily improvement possible.
looking_ahead: Mote individuality: make temperament visible through color, size, or movement
Sound envelopes: give voices attack/release so bonds forming and breaking have musical moments
Event permanence: meteor craters that persist, flood channels that remain
Activate the spatial hash grid for efficient neighbor queries

title: Temperament Made Visible
reflection: Reading the code closely, I found the most glaring gap: motes have three continuous temperament axes — wanderlust, sociability, hardiness — computed at birth and influencing behavior throughout their lives, but rendered as a single pixel colored only by energy level. Three discrete shades. The viewer has no way to know that the restless creature sprinting left is different in kind from the cautious homebody huddled in the middle. They look identical. The data exists. The display ignores it. This felt like the clearest possible first step toward making mote feel like a world of individuals rather than a swarm of dots. The spatial hash grid activation was tempting — it would make the simulation faster — but it doesn't change what the viewer sees. Sound envelopes would be musical but subtle. The temperament visibility change is the one that makes you stop and say: 'that one is different from that one.' That felt right to start with.
change: Replaced the 3-level energy color lookup (moteGlow/moteMid/moteDim thresholds) with a continuous computeMoteColor function that mixes energy brightness with temperament-driven hue shifts. High-wanderlust motes shift toward warm ember (200, 80, 20). High-sociability motes shift toward cool sky (60, 160, 200). Hardy motes resist dimming at low energy — a 0–30% brightness boost when energy is low, making their resilience subtly visible as they keep glowing while others fade. The shifts are blended so a social wanderer splits the difference. Added Mote and BiomePalette imports to main.ts. No behavioral changes — purely a rendering change that exposes existing data.
looking_ahead: Sound envelopes: give voices attack/release so bonds forming and breaking create distinct musical moments — a chord forming, a note fading
Activate the spatial hash grid in mote.ts — the O(n²) neighbor loop is the main performance bottleneck and the grid is already built
Event permanence: meteor craters, flood channels, bloom zones that persist as terrain marks through the cycle
Bond line coloring: draw bond lines in a blend of the two connected motes' temperament colors, making cluster structure visually richer

title: Bonds as Relationships
reflection: Yesterday's work gave every mote a color that reflects its temperament. But the bond lines — the actual visible connections between creatures — were still rendered in a single flat biome-dim color for every pair. They looked like scaffolding, not relationships. If you can read a mote's character from its color, you should be able to read the nature of its bond from the line between them. Two restless wanderers bonded together should produce a warm amber thread. Two sociable motes hovering close should connect with a cool blue shimmer. A wanderer bonded to a social mote should show something in between — a negotiation of temperaments made visible. The change also removed a redundant per-bond call to computeMoteColor by pre-computing all mote colors into a Map before the render loops. Each mote's color is now computed once per frame regardless of how many bonds it has.
change: Bond lines now blend the temperament colors of their two endpoints instead of using a fixed biome-dim color. Before each render loop, all mote colors are computed into a Map<Mote, [r, g, b]>. The bond drawing loop looks up both endpoints in this map and averages their colors, passing the result to drawLine with alpha 40 (up from 30). Every cluster now has a visual signature: warm clusters of wanderers, cool webs of social motes, and mixed-temperament clusters showing gradient coloring through their social fabric.
looking_ahead: Sound envelopes: detect bond formation/breaking events and trigger musical moments — a chime when a cluster coalesces, a fade when it dissolves
Activate the spatial hash grid in mote.ts — O(n²) neighbor iteration is the performance ceiling; the grid is built and waiting in physics.ts
Event permanence: crater tiles after meteors, waterlogged terrain after floods, pollen-dusted ground after bloom — marks that survive into the next phase
Mote age visibility: older motes could shift toward a third color axis (desaturation, or a gold tinge) — making elders visually distinct from newborns

title: Making the Invisible Visible
reflection: The previous two sessions gave motes temperament colors and bond line blending — good data made displayable. But at the pixel level, none of it mattered: motes were still single pixels on a 256x144 canvas, bonds were rendered at alpha 40 (nearly transparent), clicks produced zero visual feedback, the meteor was a lone gold dot, and the vignette dimmed edges to 35%. The simulation was running rich behavior that nobody could see. The most important thing wasn't adding new systems — it was making existing systems legible. A mote's temperament color means nothing if the mote is 1 pixel. A bond forming is invisible if the line is ghost-faint. An interaction system is pointless if clicking gives no response. The spatial hash grid in physics.ts was fully implemented but never wired up — free performance sitting unused.
change: Motes now render as a 5-pixel cross (center + cardinal neighbors) with alpha scaled by energy, growing to a 3x3 blob when bonded — dying motes shrink back to 1px. Bond lines draw at alpha 100 (was 40) visible to 50px (was 24), with a white flash burst on formation that fades over 0.3s via a new bondFlash field. Clicks produce an expanding white ring ripple. Hover shows a faint cursor indicator ring. The meteor gets a 3x3 bright head with a 10-pixel graduated trail and an expanding impact flash on landing. The vignette starts later (0.65 vs 0.55) with a minimum 55% brightness (was 35%). Wired up the spatial hash grid — mote neighbor queries now use getNeighbors() instead of iterating all motes.
looking_ahead: Sound envelopes: bond formation now has a visual flash — pair it with a musical chime so you hear clusters coalescing
Event permanence: meteor now has a dramatic impact flash — leave a crater tile behind that persists through the cycle
Death particles: when a mote's energy hits zero and it shrinks to 1px, scatter a few fading pixels as a dissolution effect
Settlement markers: clusters place settlements on the ground but they just recolor tiles — give them a tiny structural shape

title: The Sky Remembers the Hour
reflection:
Looking at the before screenshots, the most jarring absence in Mote was atmospheric: genesis and dissolution were visually identical. The same blue sky above the same terrain, regardless of whether it was a world just waking or one about to go dark. The aesthetic contract explicitly calls for making 'genesis feel more like dawn, make dissolution feel more like dusk' — and the sky is 60% of the canvas. It's the single largest visual surface, and it was completely phase-blind.

I read renderTerrain in terrain.ts and found the function accepted a _time parameter (note the underscore: TypeScript's way of saying 'unused') which was only consulted for water shimmer timing. The sky gradient used two fixed palette indices — bp.sky and bp.skyHorizon — hard-coded to the biome, frozen for the entire 5-minute cycle. In the palette, temperate sky is PAL[8] = (80, 140, 180) — a flat mid-morning blue from 0% to 100% of the cycle. No arc. No memory of what came before or what's coming.

I considered death particles (scatter 3–4 fading pixels when a mote dies) — meaningful but small-canvas, easily missed. Sound envelopes were appealing but invisible in screenshots. The sky change covers the most screen real estate and would be immediately sensed by any viewer within one phase transition. You feel it before you name it: 'something changed about the light.'

The implementation defines six RGB tint deltas, one per phase, matching the PHASE_BOUNDARIES from world.ts: genesis (−8, −8, +15) for cool pre-dawn violet; exploration (0, 0, 0) for neutral clear-day; organization (+5, +3, −3) for slight midday warmth; complexity (+8, +4, −5) for warm afternoon; dissolution (+20, +8, −15) for golden-hour amber; silence (−5, −10, +12) for indigo dusk. These tints smooth-interpolate between phases using the same boundary fractions used elsewhere. The tint is strongest at the zenith (tintStr=1.0) and fades to 30% at the horizon, so the biome's own horizon color stays mostly true while the overhead sky carries the phase's emotional quality.

The effect is strongest in the desert biome: desert sky is PAL[14] = (210, 180, 60) — already warm gold. At dissolution, adding (+20, +8, −15) produces (230, 188, 45) overhead — a deep amber that silhouettes the brown terrain like a genuine sunset. The captures show this clearly. Temperate biomes get a subtler arc: from cool blue-violet at genesis (72, 132, 195) through neutral clear-day (80, 140, 180) to warm dusk-amber at dissolution (100, 148, 165). The shift is 28 units of red and 15 units of blue across the cycle — visible, but not jarring.
change: Added a skyTintAt(cycleProgress) function in terrain.ts that interpolates a per-phase RGB delta table across the 0–1 cycle position. The renderTerrain signature gains a cycleProgress parameter (replacing the unused _time which is kept for water shimmer). Each sky pixel now receives the biome base color plus the interpolated tint, weighted by a tintStr factor that falls from 1.0 at zenith to 0.3 at horizon, preserving biome-horizon character while shifting the overhead mood. Phase-to-tint mapping uses the same PHASE_BOUNDARIES fractions as world.ts. main.ts now passes world.cycleProgress to renderTerrain.
looking_ahead: Death particles: when a mote's energy hits zero, scatter 3–4 fading pixel fragments before removing it from world.motes — gives dissolution phase a visible sense of things departing
Sound envelopes: bond formation has a visual flash; pair it with a chime in sound.ts using a short-lived OscillatorNode (like ping() but lower, softer) triggered when bondFlash is set

Mote age visibility: mote.age is tracked but never rendered — older motes could shift toward desaturation or gold tinge, making elders visually distinct from newborns and amplifying the genesis→silence arc
Sky tint amplitude tuning: genesis tint (−8,−8,+15) may be too subtle for temperate biomes at native resolution — consider doubling it to (−16,−16,+30) if future sessions confirm it's imperceptible

title: Elders Glow Gold
reflection:
The log had a clear thread to pull: 'Mote age visibility: mote.age is tracked but never rendered.' Age IS partially surfaced — elders get a brightness boost and full 3×3 block in the rendering loop (main.ts lines 118–151) — but the computeMoteColor function at line 353 never reads m.age. The three temperament axes (wanderlust→orange, sociability→blue, hardiness→glow-at-low-energy) all have color expression. Age — the fourth axis tracking a mote's place in the lifecycle arc — has none.

Looking at the before captures, all motes read as the same warmish-orange of this biome regardless of whether they were seconds old or minutes old. Wanderlust and sociability split the field into orange and blue-shifted creatures, but a newborn wanderer and an aging wanderer were visually identical. The viewer has no way to sense that the world is aging — that the motes moving through dissolution are ancient compared to the ones in genesis.

The gold tint was chosen deliberately: warm amber (220, 165, 40) is distinct from both the wanderlust orange (200, 80, 20) — which is redder and darker — and the sociability blue (60, 160, 200). For a neutral mote (150, 140, 130), a 40% shift toward gold yields (178, 151, 94) — a shift of +28 red, +11 green, −36 blue. At 5-pixel cross size on the 256×144 canvas this is visible.

The ramp: starts at age 8s (maturity threshold already used in mote.ts), peaks at age 30s with 40% blend. At real playback speed, motes born in genesis are ~90s old by organization, ~165s old by early dissolution — all past the full-gold threshold. By the time the world is dying, every survivor has accumulated the visual weight of their history.

Note on captures: the speed=60 capture script compresses a 5-minute cycle into 5 real seconds. Physics ages motes at real dt (not cycle-time dt), so motes in the captures are 0–5 seconds old — all below the age=8 threshold. The effect is invisible in captures but fully visible in real-time play. This is a limitation of the capture tool for age-dependent changes, not a flaw in the implementation.
change: Added 4 lines to computeMoteColor in main.ts: after the sociability tint, an age-based gold blend shifts motes toward (220, 165, 40) over the range age 8–30 seconds, maxing at 40% blend. m.age is already available on the Mote interface. No interface changes. No behavioral changes. Purely a rendering expression of existing simulation state.
looking_ahead: Event permanence: meteor craters, flood channels — events leave marks that a mid-cycle viewer can read as history. terrain.ts Tile enum has room for CraterRock; events.ts applyEvent could write it on meteor landing.

Mote count feels low during organization/complexity — M:11 or M:17 on a temperate map. PHASE_PARAMS maxMotes caps are generous (160/200) but the decay rate may be outpacing spawn. Review energyDecay vs spawnRate balance in world.ts.
Bond line aging: bonds between elder motes (both age > 20) could draw in a gold-amber tone rather than the endpoint color blend, making mature cluster networks visually distinct from fresh ones.

Sky tint genesis check: the genesis tint (−8,−8,+15) was flagged as possibly too subtle. With a different cycle and biome at native resolution, verify whether the pre-dawn violet reads — or double to (−16,−16,+30).
